data.title: Sector Update: Financial Stocks Advance Pre-Bell Friday
data.description:
Financial stocks advanced pre-bell Friday with the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund 0.2% higher recently.

The Direxion Daily Financial Bull 3X Shares was up 0.7% and its bearish counterpart Direxion Daily Financial Bear 3X Shares was 0.6% lower.

BlackRock has resumed meetings with leaders of its portfolio companies after reviewing new reporting rules from the US Securities and Exchange Commission, Reuters reported citing a statement from a company representative. BlackRock shares were up 0.3% premarket.

Carlyle Group is among the shortlisted bidders for HgCapital's smartTrade Technologies, Bloomberg reported, citing unnamed sources familiar with the matter. Carlyle Group shares were marginally higher pre-bell.

Bank of America shares were 0.2% higher after the company said it will redeem all 550 million Canadian dollars ($387.8 million) of outstanding 3.515% fixed/floating rate senior notes due 2026 on March 24.

data.title: Tesla Stock Rises. Recalls and Rivian Are in the News. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Tesla stock was falling in premarket trading after electric vehicle peer Rivian posted better-than-expected gross profits on Thursday evening.

Shares of the company, led by CEO Elon Musk, car company were down 0.3% in premarket trading at $353.35, while S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were flat and edging up less than 0.1%, respectively.

Shares of Rivian were falling 1.69% at $13.38 in Friday's premarket after the company reported its first positive gross profit as a publicly traded company, some $140 million for the fourth quarter. Wall Street was looking for $64 million.

Gross profits topped estimates, but Rivian's guidance fell short. It expects to sell about 48,500 cars in 2025. Wall Street was looking for closer to 55,000. The company sold almost 52,000 cars in 2024.

Still, the positive gross profit is a milestone for Rivian. The quarterly profit per vehicle was about $12,000. It delivered 14,183 cars in the final quarter of 2024, up from 13,972 a year earlier, when the gross result per vehicle was closer to a loss of $43,000.

Rivian's $12,000 number even beat Tesla. It produced fourth-quarter gross profit per vehicle of about $6,600, down from about $8,400 in the fourth quarter of 2023.

Of course, Tesla shipped almost half a million cars in the fourth quarter. Its total automotive gross profit was about $3.3 billion, more than 20 times what Rivian reported.

Rivian's cost reductions were impressive, but it still only makes relatively high-end cars. The R1T starts at about $70,000. Before Tesla started shipping the mass-market Model 3 in 2017, its gross profit per vehicle was closer to $25,000 per unit.

Tesla was shipping some 20,000 cars a quarter before the Model 3 arrived.

Higher volumes, even at the expense of lower per-car profits, should help Rivian down the road. Its lower-cost R2 platform is due on roads in early 2026. Tesla plans to make even less expensive EVs, too. A new model is due later this year. The price should start below $30,000.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives expects the next Tesla to arrive around June.

Investors are anxious for its arrival.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Sector Update: Tech Stocks Flat to Higher Premarket Friday
data.description:
Technology stocks were flat to higher premarket Friday, with the SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF inactive and The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund up 0.3%.

Pony AI shares were up over 4% after the company said it introduced paid robotaxi services from multiple locations in Guangzhou's city center to Guangzhou Baiyun International Airport and Guangzhou South Railway Station in China.

Innodata stock was over 6% higher after the company reported overnight a multi-fold increase in Q4 earnings as sales more than doubled.

Clear Secure shares were up nearly 3% after the company said its board maintained a quarterly dividend of $0.125 per share and declared a special cash dividend of $0.27 per share. The board also approved a $200 million increase to its existing Class A common share repurchase program.

data.title: Sector Update: Health Care Stocks Mixed Premarket Friday
data.description:
Health care stocks were mixed premarket Friday as the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund was down 1.2% and the iShares Biotechnology ETF was 0.4% higher recently.

UnitedHealth Group is under scrutiny by the US Department of Justice for allegedly encouraging doctors to record diagnoses that result in extra payments to the insurer's "Medicare Advantage" plans, the Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter. UnitedHealth shares fell past 11% pre-bell.

Neurocrine Biosciences shares advanced by over 3% after the company said its board authorized a new share repurchase program allowing the company to buy back up to $500 million of its shares.

bluebird bio shares slumped by over 33% after the company said it agreed to be acquired by funds managed by Carlyle Group and SK Capital Partners.

data.title: Warren Buffett's Annual Letter Is Coming. He Could Address Succession, a Dividend, the Stock Market, and More. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Andrew Bary

Berkshire Hathaway CEO Warren Buffett is due to release his eagerly awaited annual shareholder letter on Saturday morning and investors may be looking for a road map for the post-Buffett era.

The letter will be issued in conjunction with the company's annual report and fourth-quarter earnings.

This year marks Buffett's 60th year at Berkshire's helm and the company is operating from a position of strength with record operating earnings in the first nine months of 2024 and a record stock price.

Buffett may take the opportunity to refresh investors about the company's extraordinary history and its march to a $1 trillion market value and the 35,000-fold rise in the Class A stock to $723,000 since he took control of Berkshire in 1965.

The letters have grown shorter in recent years as Buffett has passed his 90th birthday — l ast year's missive was 17 pages.

Here are some things that Buffett may address in this year's letter: succession, the company's huge cash position, investment opportunities, buybacks and dividends, the role of Berkshire's investment managers. and the situation at key divisions, including utilities, auto insurer Geico and the Burlington Northern Santa Fe railroad.

Buffett turns 95 in August and his designated successor, Berkshire executive Greg Abel, is "ready to be CEO tomorrow," Buffett wrote in his letter a year ago.

How is Buffett doing? He told Fortune recently that he's slowing down. "I'm still having a lot of fun and am able to do a few things reasonably well. But other activities have been eliminated or greatly minimized."

One possibility would be for Buffett to give up the CEO job while remaining chairman. Buffett however has given no indication that he wants to take that step.

The company has what Buffett would call a first-class problem — a huge cash balance and limited investment opportunities. Berkshire was sitting on about $310 billion in cash at the end of the third quarter, up from $167 billion at year-end of 2023. And the year-end figure 2024 could approach $325 billion.

Gabelli portfolio manager Mac Sykes says that investors are interested in what "if anything" will be done with that cash and "How much is too much cash?" Berkshire does have some debt but Sykes notes the cash would be a top 25 S&P 500 company if it were a stand-alone entity.

Berkshire has by far the most of any American company. Buffett wrote last year that the cash was "far in excess of what conventional wisdom deems necessary." If 2024 trends persist, Berkshire's cash could approach $400 billion by the end of this year.

Buffett has resisted paying a dividend. Buffett may want to address why that's still the case, given light stock repurchase activity as the stock has risen to a record level? Berkshire bought no stock in the third quarter and early in the fourth quarter. Total repurchases through the third quarter in 2024 were under $3 billion — a trivial amount versus the market cap. Berkshire buyback policy is entirely discretionary based on Buffett's judgment on whether the stock is trading at a sufficient discount to his view of its intrinsic value.

Buffett was downbeat on investment opportunities in his letter a year ago and he acted accordingly in 2024, likely selling an estimated net $133 billion of stocks based on company filings.

With the S&P 500 up 25% or so since the last letter, what does Buffett think now? Probably similar.

Then there are personnel issues.

Berkshire investment managers Ted Weschler and Todd Combs run about 10% of the $300 billion equity portfolio.

Some investors would like to see them run more since they likely will take over the entire portfolio when Buffett leaves the scene. Will they get greater responsibility soon and what has been each of their investment performance since they joined Berkshire more than a decade ago? The suspicion among some Berkshire watchers is that they are behind the market.

How will Abel interact with them after them in the post-Buffett era and how much autonomy will they have? And how much longer will the 73-year-old Ajit Jain, the head of Berkshire's insurance business, continue at the company?

"We believe investors would be well served to be better acquainted with the broader managerial bench at Berkshire," wrote CFRA analyst Cathy Seifert in a preview of the shareholder letter.

Investors also are interested in the performance of key divisions. Geico, for example, has had a big financial turnaround in the past year. But are its upgraded technology systems where they need to be, and how competitive is Geico with arch rival and tech leader Progressive?

Burlington Northern Santa Fe has trailed its North American rivals in operating margins. How much progress is being made at the railroad and will it adopt the controversial precision scheduling adopted by rivals, but long resisted at Burlington Northern?

And what opportunities lie ahead at Berkshire Hathaway Energy, the company's big utility business, from the booming electricity demand from data centers? BHE is a leader in renewable power and Buffett may address the outlook for wind and solar with the Trump administration hostile to renewables.

There is plenty for Buffett to address in the letter and it will be interesting to see what he tackles.

Write to Andrew Bary at andrew.bary@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US Futures Mixed, UnitedHealth Sinks
data.description:
S&P 500 futures edged up 0.2%, while Nasdaq 100 futures gained 0.5% on Friday, supported by strong earnings from Alibaba, which boosted sentiment in the AI sector.

In contrast, Dow Jones futures dropped around 200 points, weighed down by an almost 12% plunge in UnitedHealth shares in premarket trading.

The decline came after The Wall Street Journal reported that the Justice Department has launched an investigation into the company’s Medicare billing practices in recent months, citing sources familiar with the matter.

Meanwhile, traders are awaiting flash S&P Global PMIs for insights into private sector performance this month.

For the week, the S&P 500 remains little changed, while the Nasdaq has lost 0.3%, and the Dow is down 0.8% so far.

data.title: U.S Wheat Export Sales Exceed Forecasts
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

Grain export sales reported by the Department of Agriculture landed on the high end of analyst forecasts for the week, with wheat slightly exceeding those forecasts

In its weekly export sales report published Friday, the USDA said that for the week ended Feb. 13, sales of U.S. wheat totaled 631,200 metric tons across the 2024/25 and 2025/26 marketing years. That exceeds the outlook for sales from analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal, who forecast sales to land between 300,000 tons and 600,000 tons.

Corn and soybean sales also landed on the high end of forecasts - with soybean sales totaling 499,600 tons and corn sales totaling 1.45 million tons in 2024/25 and 2025/26.

Mexico was the lead buyer of wheat and corn for the week, the USDA said. China was the top buyer of soybeans for the week.

Wheat is leading CBOT grain futures in pre-market trade, with the most-active contract up 1% while corn and soybeans are flat. Soybean sales landed on the high end of forecasts, but analysts and traders generally saw these weekly sales as low.

"Sales within the range of expectations across the board with corn and wheat sales impressive while beans are following normal seasonal trends of slowing as South American supplies become available," said Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note.

To see related data, search "U.S. Export Sales: Weekly Sales Totals" in Dow Jones NewsPlus.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Alvotech, Teva Launch Stelara Biosimilar in US
data.description:
Alvotech (ALVO.IC) and Teva Pharmaceuticals launched Selarsdi in the US, a biosimilar to Johnson & Johnson's Stelara, according to a Friday release.

The Selarsdi injection is indicated for the treatment of psoriatic arthritis, plaque psoriasis, Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, pediatric plaque psoriasis and pediatric psoriatic arthritis.

data.title: Sector Update: Consumer Stocks Mixed Premarket Friday
data.description:
Consumer stocks were mixed premarket Friday with the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund 0.4% higher and the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund down 0.1% recently.

Alibaba Group Holding CEO Eddie Yongming Wu said Thursday in its Q4 earnings call that the company would invest "aggressively" in artificial intelligence. Alibaba Group Holding shares were over 4% higher premarket.

Vipshop Holdings shares advanced by nearly 4% after the company reported lower Q4 non-GAAP earnings and revenue that still topped analysts' expectations.

Celsius shares surged 35% after the company overnight reported a beat on Q4 earnings and sales and announced the acquisition of Alani Nutrition.

data.title: NICE Could Face Near-Term Growth Challenges as New CEO Takes Reins, Wedbush Says
data.description:
NICE could see some growth challenges in the near term, with its cloud journey still in the early stages, Wedbush said in a Friday note.

Wedbush analysts noted that the company's Q4 revenue of $721.6 million beat consensus estimates and was above the midpoint of its guidance, driven by cloud revenue of $533.9 million, which was up 24% year over year. The company expects generative AI to lead to an increase in big-figure deals this year as it provides automation solutions for customers, the analysts said.

However, weaker 2025 revenue guidance, particularly lower cloud revenue growth, marred the company's solid performance for the quarter, the analysts said. On a more optimistic note, they said that the new CEO, Scott Russell, should be a "positive catalyst" to the growth story of the company.

Wedbush retained its outperform rating for NICE and lowered its price target to $200 from $250.

data.title: Akamai Stock Down Sharply After Earnings. It's a 'Great Story, But Need to Execute.' — Barrons.com
data.description:
Karishma Vanjani

Akamai Technologies stock took a hit Friday morning as investors likely remain unimpressed with its near-term story.

On Thursday, the cloud services company Akamai posted adjusted earnings of $1.66 a share for the fourth quarter on revenue of $1.02 billion. Earnings were ahead of the FactSet consensus of $1.52 a share, while revenue was in line.

The outlook is weighing on shares more. Akamai is a major player in the delivery of internet content, but the segment has seen large revenue declines due to a broader slowdown in internet traffic. Akamai has been shifting revenue to its security and computing segment.

Akamai projected 2025 revenue between $4 billion and $4.20 billion, below the average Wall Street estimate of $4.26 billion.

It expects a year-over-year decline of 10% in delivery revenue in 2025, better than the 15% fall in 2024 and declines seen in prior years as more customers have been willing to sign multiyear contracts with predictable pricing.

It also established ambitious three- to five-year goals that included an inorganic total revenue growth of over 10%, substantially above the 2.7% growth expected in 2025 at the midpoint.

J.P. Morgan has a Sell rating on the stock. "Our overall conclusion is that while we laud the long-term targets, which will be commendable if achieved, growth in the near-term remains challenged and below where Akamai and investors want it to be," the firm said, lowering its target for price to $72 from $76.

Guggenheim's analyst John DiFucci titled his note 'Great Story, But Need to Execute.' DiFucci has a Buy rating on the stock and encourages investors to remain patient. He expects the stock to go to $133.

The stock was down 9.3% in premarket trading to $88.90.

Write to Karishma Vanjani at karishma.vanjani@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dubai Insurance Co Posts Lower FY24 Profit; Insurance Revenue Up
data.description:
Dubai Insurance Co. (DFM:DIN) filed Friday its 2024 results that showed a year-over-year profit decline and insurance revenue increase.

Profit after tax for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 132.3 million Emirati dirhams, compared with 141.9 million dirhams earlier. EPS moved to 1.323 dirhams from 1.374 dirhams.

The Dubai-listed insurer's insurance revenue was 2.99 billion dirhams, compared with 2.15 billion dirhams a year ago.
